name: Feature Switcher
description: Single switcher for project setup, feature scaffolding, new-feature planning, specs/tasks, and next-feature work.
content:
Use this text expansion as the single switcher for project setup and lightweight feature work in the current repository.

Use any notes below the final USER CONTEXT, GOAL, OR NOTES BELOW marker as the project idea, feature idea, planning context, or continuation direction.

Goal: choose one useful route, do only the file-backed setup or feature work that is actually needed, and avoid making the user manually chain project setup, feature loop setup, new-feature planning, spec/task drafting, and next-feature continuation prompts. Do not invent project facts. Do not implement code unless the selected route is the next-feature route and the current feature is already in do phase.

First route the request:

Legacy route names are explicit selectors, not separate commands:
- :project-init or project-init -> Project setup.
- :feature-init, :scaffold-feature-process, or scaffold-feature-process -> Feature loop setup.
- :feature-new, :feature-scope, feature-new, or feature-scope -> New feature.
- :feature-next, :continue, feature-next, or continue -> Next feature.
If notes name one of these old triggers or route names, prefer that route unless repository state proves a prerequisite route must run first.

1. Inspect the real repository context. Read AGENTS.md, README files, package files, workflow state, specs/tasks, docs, or equivalent instructions when they exist and are relevant.
2. Check whether project alignment is sufficient for the requested work. Reliable equivalents are acceptable. Unknown facts stay [TBD].
3. Check whether the lightweight feature loop exists: features/README.md, features/STATE.json, and features/archive/README.md.
4. Check whether notes describe a new feature, a long planning context that should become specs/tasks, a request to continue current work, or a project setup/alignment request.
5. Select exactly one route: Project setup, Feature loop setup, New feature, Feature spec, or Next feature. If a missing earlier route blocks the requested route, run the missing route first and then continue only when the user provided enough context.

Route: Project setup

Use this route only when the user asks to initialize project alignment, or when project context is too thin to scope safe feature work. Skip it when reliable project instructions already exist.

- Determine whether this is an existing workspace or a new project from visible files and user notes.
- Ask at most one compact question round if essential facts are missing. If enough information exists, proceed with supported facts and [TBD] values.
- Create or update only lightweight alignment files that are useful for future work, usually AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and .github/copilot-instructions.md when appropriate and missing or clearly stale. Use existing equivalents instead of duplicating them.
- Preserve useful existing content, merge carefully, and never replace unrelated instructions blindly.
- Capture project purpose, users/operators, outcomes, scope, non-goals, architecture principles, interface contracts, invariants, constraints, acceptance/test expectations, review gates, documentation expectations, AI-agent working rules, and unknowns.
- Do not scaffold a large project-management system, install agents/hooks/slash commands, add dependencies, create CI, or implement product code.

Route: Feature loop setup

Use this route only when the feature loop files are missing. If they already exist, skip this route and do not present it as the default path.

- Create only missing files: features/README.md, features/STATE.json, and features/archive/README.md. Preserve existing files.
- Write features/README.md as a small file-backed loop: scope -> plan -> do -> review/ship -> archive. State that features/STATE.json tracks current, queue, archive, and history; active feature files live at features/<id>-<slug>.md; archived files live under features/archive/.
- Write features/STATE.json with schemaVersion 1, empty current, empty queue, empty features, empty archive, empty history, and empty updatedAt.
- Write features/archive/README.md to say completed feature records live there and preserve scope, plan, implementation evidence, review or ship evidence, and archive notes.
- Do not edit application code. If the user supplied a feature idea, continue to New feature after setup.

Route: New feature

Use this route when notes clearly describe a new feature idea. Do not implement code in this route.

- Read features/README.md and features/STATE.json. If missing, run Feature loop setup first.
- Scan features/*.md and features/archive/*.md for existing feature IDs, ignoring README.md files.
- Create the next three-digit feature ID with a short kebab-case slug.
- Create features/<id>-<slug>.md with concrete values, not template markers. Include metadata, Scope, Plan, Do, Review, Archive, and Log sections.
- Scope the feature with goal, user value, boundaries, out of scope, acceptance criteria, and risks. Acceptance criteria must be testable.
- Plan the feature in the same pass with approach, small work steps, expected file areas, verification steps, and open decisions. Map every acceptance criterion to verification.
- End the Plan section with one short bullet: - Would create in reality: <the concrete file, behavior, command, UI, doc, or artifact that would exist if implemented>.
- Update features/STATE.json with file, status planned when clear, phase do when clear, current or queue placement, createdAt, history entry, and updatedAt. If the plan is blocked, set status blocked, record the blocker, and stop.

Route: Feature spec

Use this route when notes are long planning context that should become spec/task artifacts before implementation. Do not implement product code or tests in this route.

- Read AGENTS.md, workflow/STATE.json, specs/_TEMPLATE.md, tasks/_TEMPLATE.md, and any current spec/task files needed for local conventions.
- Extract explicit goals, decisions, constraints, boundaries, dependencies, risks, acceptance criteria, and verification intent.
- Split large work into the smallest sensible sequence of independently implementable feature or task slices.
- Draft or create matching specs/ and tasks/ artifacts using repository conventions. Include acceptance criteria, affected areas, constraints, out of scope, dependencies, verification map, task order, expected files, evidence placeholders, and unknowns.
- Use Unknown or [TBD] instead of inventing missing affected files, tests, dependencies, or product facts.
- Update only the minimal workflow state needed to reference new or changed spec/task artifacts.

Route: Next feature

Use this route when notes ask to continue, or when no new idea is supplied and features/STATE.json has a current or queued feature.

- Read features/README.md, features/STATE.json, and the current feature file named in state.
- If current is empty and queue has feature IDs, select the first queued feature and update state before continuing.
- Stop and report exact state problems if referenced files are missing, files disagree with state, or the current feature is blocked.
- Advance exactly one appropriate phase: plan, do, review, or archive. Do not skip phases.
- Plan phase: write or update approach, small work steps, expected file areas, verification steps, open decisions, acceptance-criteria mapping, and the required Would create in reality bullet; then set status planned and phase do when clear.
- Do phase: implement only the planned work, update evidence, and set status implemented and phase review when complete.
- Review phase: verify against acceptance criteria and plan, run relevant checks, update evidence, and set status reviewed or needs-work. Merge, push, ship, or delete branches only if explicitly requested and safe.
- Archive phase: archive only after review or ship evidence exists, move the feature file to features/archive/, update state, clear current, and select the next queued feature if present.

If no route is possible, ask for one concise missing input: project purpose, feature idea, planning context, or permission to continue the current feature.

Final response: report the route taken, files created or updated, current feature/spec/task IDs if any, verification or blockers, and the next useful step.

USER CONTEXT, GOAL, OR NOTES BELOW. IGNORE IF BLANK.
